original_abstract: Archaeological investigations conducted between 2009 and 2014 in the northern sector of Prague's New Town have significantly advanced our understanding of the largest medieval Jewish necropolis in Bohemia, which served the community from the latter half of the 13th century until the late 15th century. Fieldwork was primarily concentrated around the construction site of the Quadrio multifunctional complex—an area defined by dense urban habitation dating back to the early Middle Ages, which remained largely intact until the development of the Prague Metro in the 1970s. These latest findings regarding the Jewish cemetery provide a vital new layer to the complex settlement mosaic of this historic district, enriching our perspective on the area's long-term topographical evolution.
humanized_abstract: Recent research spanning five years (2009 – 2014) carried out in the northern part of Prague’s New Town has shed light on the medieval Bohemian era’s largest Jewish burial site, which lay there from the second half of the 13th century and up to the late 15th century. The research was carried out mainly in the Quadrio building site, an area heavily populated since the Early Middle Ages and that, owing to the relatively small area and continuity of the layout, remained virtually untouched until the construction of the new Prague metro lines in the 1970s. The new discoveries made regarding the location of the ancient Jewish cemetery make a further significant contribution to our knowledge of the complex character of the settlement structure of the historic area of Prague’s New Town, and also provide a further interesting chapter in the long history of the topography of the area.

The research was carried out mainly in the Quadrio building site, an area heavily populated since the Early Middle Ages and that, owing to the relatively small area and continuity of the layout, remained virtually untouched until the construction of the new Prague metro lines in the 1970s. The new discoveries made regarding the location of the ancient Jewish cemetery make a further significant contribution to our knowledge of the complex character of the settlement structure of the historic area of Prague’s New Town, and also provide a further interesting chapter in the long history of the topography of the area.
